cards.title: In-Memory Database Mode
cards.teaser: A DuckDB in-memory database lives entirely in RAM — fast, zero file I/O, and completely gone when the connection closes.

runs.text: The simplest way to open DuckDB is with no file at all:

explanation.code:
import duckdb

con = duckdb.connect(':memory:')
con.execute("CREATE TABLE events (id INTEGER, name VARCHAR)")
con.execute("INSERT INTO events VALUES (1, 'click')")

runs.text: Or equivalently — the default when you call
with no arguments is also in-memory.

items.text: Create a fresh schema, run your test, and discard it. No cleanup needed.
Read a file, transform it, write the result somewhere else. The intermediate state does not need to survive.
Spin up a connection in a notebook, query a few CSVs, and throw the connection away.
No disk writes, no leftover files, no cleanup step.

runs.text: The lifecycle is explicit: data exists as long as the connection object exists. When the connection is garbage-collected or
is called, all data is gone.
Memory implications: in-memory mode still uses DuckDB's vectorized engine and will spill to temporary disk files if a query exceeds the memory limit.
means "no persistent file," not "guaranteed to fit in RAM."
calls create two completely isolated databases — they do not share state. If you need multiple connections to the same in-memory database, use
(no argument) and share the connection object.
Like an in-memory SQLite database — it is a fully functional database engine with no backing file, useful for exactly the same reasons: fast tests, throwaway transforms, and ephemeral state.

cards.title: File-Backed Database
cards.teaser: Pass a file path to `duckdb.connect()` and DuckDB creates a persistent `.duckdb` file that survives process restarts and is portable across platforms.

runs.text: Creating or opening a persistent database is identical to in-memory — you just supply a path:

explanation.code: con = duckdb.connect('analytics.duckdb')

-- From the CLI:
duckdb analytics.duckdb

runs.text: If the file does not exist, DuckDB creates it. If it does exist, DuckDB opens it. The same path argument works across Python, the CLI, Node.js, Go, and every other binding.

items.text: — all table definitions, views, macros, and sequences.
— all rows, stored in DuckDB's columnar PAX format.
— internal metadata linking it all together.
Data committed via a transaction is durable once
returns (or once an auto-committed statement completes).
file is a standalone file — copy it to another machine and DuckDB opens it without any migration step.
The format is platform-independent. A file created on macOS opens on Linux or Windows.

runs.text: DuckDB 1.0 stabilized the storage format — a
file created by DuckDB 1.0+ can be opened by any later 1.x version without migration. Pin to DuckDB >=1.0 in production pipelines to benefit from this guarantee.
file — a single, self-contained, portable binary file that is the entire database, no server or import step required.

cards.title: The Write-Ahead Log (WAL)
cards.teaser: DuckDB writes changes to a `.wal` file before applying them to the main database file — that file is your durability guarantee and must travel with the `.duckdb` file.

runs.text: When you write to a DuckDB file-backed database, changes are first appended to a write-ahead log — a file named
— before being applied to the main

items.text: is written to the WAL atomically.
The WAL is flushed to disk before DuckDB acknowledges the commit.
Periodically — and always on clean close — DuckDB
: it applies the WAL's contents to the main file and truncates the WAL.
If a process is killed mid-write, the WAL file will be present on disk. The next time you open the database, DuckDB replays the WAL automatically. This is correct behavior, not corruption.
file, you may lose uncommitted data or open an inconsistent snapshot.

explanation.code:
# Both files must travel together if a WAL is present
cp analytics.duckdb analytics.duckdb.wal /backup/

runs.text: You can force a checkpoint manually:

explanation.code: CHECKPOINT;

runs.text: , the WAL is empty and only the
file to git without first running
or verifying the WAL is empty. Add
for databases that are checked in.
Like a database transaction log in PostgreSQL — writes go to the log first for durability, then are periodically folded into the main data files during a checkpoint.

cards.title: Single-Writer Concurrency Limit
cards.teaser: DuckDB enforces one writer at a time per database file — attempting a second concurrent write fails immediately with a clear error, not silently.

runs.text: . This is enforced at the file level via an OS-level exclusive lock.
What happens with concurrent write attempts:

explanation.code:
# Process A
con_a = duckdb.connect('analytics.duckdb')  # acquires write lock

# Process B (separate process, simultaneous)
con_b = duckdb.connect('analytics.duckdb')
# raises: duckdb.IOException: Could not set lock on file "analytics.duckdb":
# Resource temporarily unavailable

runs.text: The lock is released when the connection is closed or the process exits.

items.text: open read-only connections (see the next card) for readers while the writer holds the lock.
funnel writes through a single worker process. Use a queue (Redis, a message bus, a simple file queue) to serialize writes.
instead of continuous writes from multiple sources, batch data externally and write in a single scheduled job.
DuckDB's managed cloud offering handles multi-writer coordination at the platform level.

runs.text: The single-writer constraint is not a bug — it is a deliberate tradeoff. Without a server to arbitrate writes, file-level locking is the only safe mechanism. The result is simple, predictable behavior at the cost of write concurrency.

Within a single process, a single DuckDB connection is thread-safe. Multiple threads can execute queries on the same connection concurrently — DuckDB parallelizes them internally. The single-writer constraint applies across
Like a write lock on a shared file in a distributed system — only one holder at a time, everyone else waits or is rejected, which avoids corruption at the cost of throughput.

cards.title: Read-Only Mode
cards.teaser: Open a DuckDB file in read-only mode to query it safely from multiple processes simultaneously, even while another process holds the write lock.

runs.text: Read-only connections do not acquire the exclusive write lock, which means multiple processes can open the same
file simultaneously as long as all of them are read-only:

explanation.code: con = duckdb.connect('analytics.duckdb', read_only=True)

-- CLI read-only flag
duckdb -readonly analytics.duckdb

items.text: Multiple dashboard instances querying the same database file simultaneously.
A reporting process reading data while a writer loads new data (reads see a consistent snapshot via MVCC).
Opening a database file that you do not have write permission to on the filesystem.

runs.text: If you attempt a write in read-only mode, DuckDB raises an error immediately.

explanation.code:
con = duckdb.connect('analytics.duckdb', read_only=True)
con.execute("INSERT INTO events VALUES (2, 'scroll')")
# raises: duckdb.TransactionException: Attempt to execute write query in read-only transaction

runs.text: In a web service that serves analytical queries, open the DuckDB connection in read-only mode at application startup. This lets you run multiple worker processes (Gunicorn workers, Uvicorn workers) against the same database file without coordination overhead — as long as a separate writer process handles updates.
in POSIX — multiple readers can hold it simultaneously, and the OS prevents anyone in read-only mode from accidentally corrupting the shared state.

cards.title: ATTACH — Multiple Databases in One Session
cards.teaser: `ATTACH` lets you open additional DuckDB files (or other data sources) inside an existing session and query across them with standard SQL.

runs.text: DuckDB supports attaching multiple databases to a single session. Each attached database is accessible by its alias, and you can join across them as if they were schemas in the same database.
The default (first) database is always accessible as
(for in-memory) or by its file path alias. Attached databases are addressed as

explanation.code:
import duckdb

con = duckdb.connect('primary.duckdb')
con.execute("ATTACH 'secondary.duckdb' AS sec")
con.execute("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM sec.main.orders")
ATTACH 'archive.duckdb' AS archive (READ_ONLY);

items.text: SQLite files via the SQLite extension:

runs.text: Cross-database queries in DuckDB run entirely within the same process. There is no network hop, no serialization, and no external query planner — the DuckDB optimizer sees all attached databases equally and can push predicates and projections across database boundaries.
in MySQL but more powerful — instead of switching databases, you mount multiple databases simultaneously and treat them like schemas in one unified namespace.

cards.title: Detaching Databases and Inspecting the Catalog
cards.teaser: `DETACH` releases an attached database and `SHOW DATABASES` gives you the current attach state — both are essential for dynamic multi-database workflows.

runs.text: Once you are done with an attached database, detach it to release the file lock:

explanation.code: DETACH warehouse;

runs.text: , the alias is gone and the write lock on
is released. The primary database (the one you opened with

explanation.code: SHOW DATABASES;

runs.text: Returns the alias and file path for each attached database. In Python:

explanation.code:
print(con.execute("SHOW DATABASES").fetchdf())
#    database_name          path
# 0       primary  primary.duckdb
# 1           sec  secondary.duckdb

runs.text: to inspect tables across attached databases:
Use cases for attaching and detaching dynamically:

items.text: attach a per-month archive database for a date-range query, detach when done.
attach source and destination databases, copy data, detach source.
attach scratch databases for intermediate work, merge results, detach.

runs.text: In long-running processes, detach databases you no longer need. Each attached file holds a file lock — accumulating open handles causes resource leaks and blocks other processes from writing.
Like mounting and unmounting filesystems in Linux —

cards.title: Copying Data Between Attached Databases
cards.teaser: With two databases attached, you can move or copy tables between them using standard SQL — no intermediate files, no export/import cycle.

runs.text: Once two databases are attached, moving data between them is plain SQL:
The entire operation runs in-process — DuckDB reads from the source database and writes to the destination database within the same query execution, using vectorized batch processing.
For larger copies, wrapping in a transaction improves performance and gives you atomicity:
If the source database is very large, consider copying in batches:

explanation.code:
-- Chunked insert to avoid memory pressure
INSERT INTO dst.main.events
SELECT * FROM src.main.events
WHERE event_date BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31';

INSERT INTO dst.main.events
SELECT * FROM src.main.events
WHERE event_date BETWEEN '2024-04-01' AND '2024-06-30';

runs.text: will fail if the table already exists in the destination. Use
across schemas in PostgreSQL — when two databases are visible to the same session, moving data between them is just SQL with a qualified table name.

cards.title: EXPORT DATABASE — Portable Snapshots
cards.teaser: `EXPORT DATABASE` dumps a DuckDB database to a directory of Parquet files and SQL schema — human-readable, portable, and readable by any Parquet-compatible tool.

runs.text: writes the entire contents of a database to a directory:

explanation.code: EXPORT DATABASE '/tmp/my_export';

items.text: — DDL for all tables, views, sequences, and macros
— a script that re-imports everything into a fresh DuckDB database

explanation.code: IMPORT DATABASE '/tmp/my_export';

runs.text: Or manually from the load script:

explanation.code: duckdb fresh.duckdb < /tmp/my_export/load.sql

items.text: Parquet files are readable by Spark, Polars, pandas, BigQuery, Snowflake, and any other Parquet-compatible tool — no DuckDB required to consume the export.
If a DuckDB major version breaks the storage format (unlikely with 1.x but possible with 2.x), export/import provides a format-agnostic migration path.
You can open individual Parquet files with any Parquet reader to inspect the data without loading the entire database.
The export directory is a self-contained backup that can be restored independently of the original

explanation.code:
-- Export with Parquet compression options
EXPORT DATABASE '/tmp/my_export' (FORMAT PARQUET, COMPRESSION ZSTD);

runs.text: exports committed data only — it does not capture in-flight transactions or WAL entries. Run
before exporting if you want to ensure all recent writes are included.
for PostgreSQL — a human-inspectable snapshot of the database that can be restored, moved, or fed into a completely different tool without needing the original binary format.

cards.title: Temporary Tables and Temporary Files
cards.teaser: `CREATE TEMP TABLE` creates a session-scoped table that is automatically dropped when the connection closes, with no impact on the persistent database.

runs.text: Temporary tables are a lightweight alternative to creating permanent tables for intermediate work:

explanation.code:
CREATE TEMP TABLE staging AS
SELECT *
FROM read_parquet('raw/*.parquet')
WHERE status = 'active';

-- Use it in subsequent queries this session
SELECT region, COUNT(*) FROM staging GROUP BY region;

-- Automatically dropped when the connection closes

runs.text: Key properties of temp tables in DuckDB:

items.text: Visible only to the current connection. No other connection sees them.
stored in a temporary file on disk (not purely in-memory), so they can exceed available RAM by spilling to disk.
Changes to temp tables are not written to the WAL and are not durable.
Multi-step transformations where the intermediate result is expensive to recompute.
Staging data before validation and insert into a permanent table.
Breaking up a complex query into debuggable steps.

runs.text: You can also explicitly drop a temp table before the session ends:

explanation.code: DROP TABLE staging;

runs.text: over creating and dropping regular tables in a workflow — it is explicit about lifecycle intent and avoids accidentally polluting the persistent schema.
Like a variable declared inside a function scope in Python — it exists for the duration of the call, is invisible outside of it, and is cleaned up automatically when the scope exits.

cards.title: Memory vs Disk: How DuckDB Spills
cards.teaser: DuckDB does not fail with OOM on large queries — it spills intermediate data to a temp directory automatically, trading speed for the ability to process data larger than RAM.

runs.text: DuckDB's memory manager enforces a configurable memory limit and automatically spills to disk when that limit is reached during a query:

items.text: Sort operations that exceed the memory limit spill sort runs to temp files.
Hash join build sides that exceed the limit spill partitions to disk.
Aggregation hash tables that exceed the limit use external aggregation.

runs.text: By default, DuckDB uses the system temp directory. On machines where the temp directory is on a slow disk (or a small tmpfs), pointing it at a faster path meaningfully improves spill performance.

items.text: Queries that fit in memory run significantly faster than those that spill.
Spill is disk I/O — query time will increase, sometimes dramatically on slow disks.
You can avoid spill by setting a higher memory limit (
) or by reducing data volume earlier in the pipeline (filter before join, project early).

runs.text: DuckDB defaults to 80% of available RAM as the memory limit. On a shared machine or in a container, set
explicitly to avoid starving other processes.
Like how a database's sort operator in PostgreSQL uses
before spilling to disk — DuckDB applies the same mechanism across joins, aggregations, and sorts, and the temp directory is where it lands.

cards.title: Checkpointing — When the WAL Is Flushed
cards.teaser: DuckDB checkpoints automatically on clean close and at a configurable WAL size threshold — understanding when checkpoints happen prevents surprises with file sizes and recovery.

runs.text: A checkpoint in DuckDB means: apply all WAL entries to the main
file, then truncate the WAL. After a checkpoint, only the
Checkpoints happen automatically in three situations:

items.text: or the connection object is garbage-collected, DuckDB checkpoints before closing.
When the WAL exceeds a threshold (default: 16MB), DuckDB triggers a background checkpoint mid-session.

runs.text: You can tune the WAL threshold:

explanation.code: SET wal_autocheckpoint = '64MB';

runs.text: Forcing a checkpoint before copying or backing up the database file:

explanation.code:
con.execute("CHECKPOINT")
# Now safe to copy analytics.duckdb — no WAL to worry about
import shutil
shutil.copy('analytics.duckdb', '/backup/analytics.duckdb')

runs.text: What happens if a checkpoint fails mid-way:

items.text: DuckDB does not corrupt the main file — the WAL is only truncated after the checkpoint is confirmed complete.
On the next open, DuckDB replays the WAL from the beginning.

runs.text: file appears smaller than expected after heavy writes, it is likely because data is in the WAL and not yet checkpointed. Run
and then check the file size again.
Like compacting a write-ahead log in Kafka or an LSM tree — you periodically merge the log entries into the base data structure so the log stays small and reads are fast.

cards.title: Database Aliases and the Default Database
cards.teaser: The first database opened in a session is the default — understanding how DuckDB resolves unqualified table names across attached databases prevents subtle query errors.

runs.text: When you open a DuckDB connection, the first database is the
prefix) resolve to the default database's

explanation.code:
con = duckdb.connect('primary.duckdb')
# primary.duckdb is the default database, aliased as "primary" (or the filename stem)

con.execute("ATTACH 'secondary.duckdb' AS sec")
# sec is now also available

con.execute("SELECT * FROM orders")
# resolves to primary.main.orders — the default database

con.execute("SELECT * FROM sec.main.orders")
# explicit cross-database reference

runs.text: You can check the current default:

explanation.code: SELECT current_database();

runs.text: You can change the default schema (within the default database):

explanation.code:
SET search_path = 'reporting';
SELECT * FROM orders;  -- now resolves to primary.reporting.orders

runs.text: You cannot change which attached database is the default mid-session — it is set at connection open time.
Common mistake: attaching a second database and assuming unqualified names search both databases. They do not — unqualified names only resolve against the default database and the

If two attached databases have a table with the same name and you use an unqualified reference, DuckDB resolves to the default database — silently. Always use qualified names in cross-database queries.
— the database has a default resolution order, unqualified names go there first, and explicit qualification is the only safe way to reference objects in non-default schemas across databases.
